_comment: Auto-generated by tools/build.mjs. Do not hand-edit. Run `npm run build`.

drafts.title: Prawn Shells, Fermented Into Umami
drafts.lede: A 2026 Singapore study fed prawn by-products to six microbes. One mould turned shell-and-head waste into a glutamate load worth six grams of MSG per hundred.

drafts.title: The Green Purée: Holding the Colour
drafts.lede: A bright, spoon-coating green purée that stays green from pass to plate. The whole trick is a hard blanch, a hard chill, and never letting acid near it until the last second.

drafts.title: Cultivated Foie Gras Nears the Galley
drafts.lede: A duck liver grown from cells — no bird force-fed — cleared its first market in 2026. For a charter galley that plates foie gras by the kilo, the question is shifting from whether to which guest asks first.

drafts.title: The Preference Sheet: Reading the Document That Runs the Job
drafts.lede: A dislike is a preference; an intolerance is a safety brief. The sheet rarely tells you which — so you learn to read it like a chart, not a wish list.

drafts.title: The Sorrento Lemon
drafts.lede: A single oval lemon from terraced cliffs above the Tyrrhenian, its peel more than seventy per cent limonene — and why a yacht chef should buy it by name.

drafts.title: The Tuna Bloodline
drafts.lede: The dark stripe most cooks trim and bin is the most concentrated flavour on the fish. Treat it right and it becomes seasoning, not waste.

drafts.title: Two Jobs, One Salary: The Chef-Stew Hybrid, Decoded
drafts.lede: ’80 percent cook, 20 percent stew’ is not a splash of extra duties. It is two positions paid as one, and the rest-hours ceiling is where it breaks.

drafts.title: Thawing Fish Without the Drip: The Cold-Brine Method
drafts.lede: Air-thawing costs you colour and up to a third of the fish’s water. A cold salt bath thaws faster, holds the flesh red, and keeps the surface out of the danger zone.

drafts.title: The Guacamole That Does Not Brown
drafts.lede: The lime is not just for taste. Drop the pH under four and the enzyme that turns guacamole grey stops working.

drafts.title: Miso From Scratch: Koji, Salt, and Time
drafts.lede: Buy a paste and you buy someone else’s decisions. Build it from koji and you control the salt, the sweetness, and how much glutamate the mould sets free.

drafts.title: The Cheap Fish Go First: Sardine and Anchovy Before Bluefin
drafts.lede: A Horizon forecast: in the north-western Mediterranean the cheap oily fish that feed the crew have the shakier decade ahead, while eastern Atlantic bluefin sits at its highest spawning biomass since the 1960s. Three named signals, a falsifiable call, and the provisioning moves to make now.

drafts.title: The Menu Agent: Turn Any AI Into a Superyacht Menu Designer
drafts.lede: A ~985-line prompt turns Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini into a superyacht menu designer: every menu anchored to a named touchstone, a self-improving palette memory so no two cards repeat, a non-overridable validation gate, and a one-page card you print from any browser.

drafts.title: MSG, Honestly: Using Glutamate to Cut the Salt Without Losing the Flavour
drafts.lede: Few ingredients carry a worse reputation on weaker evidence than monosodium glutamate. It is the most-studied way to cut a dish's sodium without flattening it — and the science that condemned it never held up. Here is what glutamate actually is, the salt it saves on a long charter, and the one honest caveat worth keeping.

drafts.title: The Pairing Does the Healing
drafts.lede: A single ingredient rarely heals. The body absorbs most active compounds only as members of a team — and the cook decides whether the team turns up. This is the working chef's guide to the combinations that actually reach the blood, the ones that quietly cancel each other, the heat that…

drafts.title: How Many Senses You Have, and How Many Sit Down to Eat
drafts.lede: The "five senses" is a 2,300-year-old shorthand, not the instrument panel your guest is eating with. Here is the honest count, and the eight or more senses that actually build a plate of flavour.

drafts.title: The Smell of Cooking: Why Onions Seduce, Broccoli Turns, and Aromas Linger
drafts.lede: A pan of onions browning in fat is the most reliable seduction in any galley — and a sprout boiled five minutes too long is the fastest way to lose a saloon. Both are the same kind of event: trace molecules, detected at concentrations your kitchen scale could never weigh, read by a nose built to care. Here is the chemistry of cooking aroma — why the good smells pull, why the bad ones repel, and why neither leaves your curtains when you ask.

drafts.title: Eggs: What Every Dish Asks of Them
drafts.lede: When to stop whisking, when to salt, what heat does to the white before the yolk — the egg, decoded dish by dish.

drafts.title: Flavours That Stay in the Mouth
drafts.lede: Aged Comte, chorizo, Epoisses, vin jaune, absinthe — why some flavours sit in the mouth long after the bite, and how to build that persistence on purpose.

drafts.title: The Parting Gift: What Guests Take Home From the Galley
drafts.lede: What to send a guest home with - the small, edible, personal thing that outlasts the charter.

drafts.title: The Parmigiano Wheel: Tableside Theatre, Judged
drafts.lede: Parmigiano wheels, bamboo steamers, dushi boats, slate, salt blocks - the serving vessel is the first thing the guest tastes with their eyes.

drafts.title: The Burger Grind
drafts.lede: Pre-ground mince is one muscle, one fat level, ground to mush. The galley fix is three cuts, two grinds and a thermometer — a patty with the bite, fat and beef depth a single-cut grind can’t reach.

drafts.title: The Preservative Paradox
drafts.lede: A 112,000-person study just linked eight common food preservatives — including added citric acid and ascorbic acid — to higher blood pressure and heart risk. What it means for a galley that runs on long-life provisioning, and what it doesn’t.

drafts.title: Whiplash: A Fermented Hot Sauce With a Sting
drafts.lede: A lacto-fermented hot sauce engineered for a clean, building heat - the one the crew steals from the galley.

drafts.title: Cooking the Atlantic Crossing: The Galley Playbook
drafts.lede: Three weeks of provisioning, twenty crew, two thousand miles and a galley that pitches. An ocean crossing is won before you leave the dock — the freezer strategy, the rough-weather meals, and the morale that quietly runs on baking and snacks.

drafts.title: Cook the Port: Terroir as a Galley Workflow
drafts.lede: Most ports hand you one ingredient worth building the menu around. Cooking the place you are in — terroir as a workflow, not a romance — makes provisioning easier, the plate better, and the markup in Monaco optional.

drafts.title: Cooking for the Ozempic Guest
drafts.lede: More guests now board on a GLP-1 drug, and a rich tasting menu is exactly the wrong plan. The science of why appetite collapses on Ozempic — and the small, low-fat, protein-first service that keeps them comfortable, and fed.

drafts.title: The Equipment Verdict: What Survives a Season at Sea
drafts.lede: The galley runs on a handful of machines, and the fleet has hard opinions earned at sea. The gear that survives a season, the gear that dies on arrival day, and the one rule that keeps you cooking when a unit fails mid-charter.

drafts.title: Sourdough at Sea: The Science-First Loaf
drafts.lede: An open, blistered crumb and a deep mahogany crust — raised on a jar of flour and water you keep alive yourself. The wild loaf made reliable on a moving galley, where the acidity that flavours it also makes it keep. Interactive: scale it, tick it, cook it.

drafts.title: What Yacht Chefs Actually Earn: A 2026 Pay Map
drafts.lede: The honest 2026 yacht-chef salary map: base pay by vessel size, every package perk priced, the guests-aboard workload, the job ranked by demand, and the modifiers (solo vs teamed, private vs charter, experience) that set your number. Plus a build-your-number worksheet.

drafts.title: elBulli: The Coastline That Rewrote Cooking
drafts.lede: A beach bar named after two French bulldogs became the most influential kitchen of the last century — then closed at its peak to protect the one thing it was built for. The frontier elBulli explored, the chemistry it left behind, and what a galley can still steal from it.

drafts.title: Sea Urchin: The Candy of the Sea
drafts.lede: Uni is a reproductive organ that tastes of sugar — and the reason why is two amino acids, glycine and alanine. The full profile: the candy chemistry, the alum trick that hides age, the four ways to use it, and how to keep something this perishable alive on charter.

drafts.title: Koji: The Mould That Cooks For You
drafts.lede: One microbe turns rice into sweetness, protein into umami, and a tough cut into something tender — before a flame is ever lit. Meet the fungus Japan made its national organism.

drafts.title: The One-Hour Lunch: Market Meals That Feel Special on Provisioning Day
drafts.lede: Provisioning day, one hour to plate lunch, a galley full of boxes. The move isn’t to cook faster — it’s to let the market do the prep and assemble with intention. Here’s the playbook.

drafts.title: Signal Fire: One Chef, One Idea That Changes Everything
drafts.lede: Signal Fire: one chef, one idea that changes everything. Not a profile. A dissection of a single principle that survived decades in kitchens. Coming soon.

drafts.title: The Locker: Deep Pantry Guides for Chefs Who Stock Like an Arsenal
drafts.lede: The Locker: deep pantry guides for chefs who stock like an arsenal. Specific brands, specific reasons, three tiers. Coming soon.
